presenterClickPath.action: Let's go to the Test Track

0.1.headline: Meet Miles.
0.1.subhead: An AI companion for Honda intrapreneurs.

milesMessages.text: Hey Ian — here's where your electric lawnmower project stands today. I pulled together everything that landed across your inbox, Slack, and project log this week. Before we pick a thread: what's the one thing you most want to move forward?

summaryCards.label: 3 emails
summaryCards.tooltip: Prototype feedback: runtime · swap-battery logistics · cut quality on tall/wet grass · weight & maneuverability · deck width vs. productivity · noise (positive). Follow-up — Laura (sole-op landscaper): Ran the demo on 3 lawns Tuesday. Got through ~2.5 properties before charge dropped — needs a full morning on one charge or a battery she can swap mid-route. So quiet her 7am client didn't complain for the first time. Fix the runtime and she's in.

1.1.promptMessage: Where do you want to start? I've got a recommendation if you want one.

inputModes.label: Voice
inputModes.tooltip: Voice and Sandbox modes aren't in this preview.

inputModes.label: Sandbox
inputModes.tooltip: Voice and Sandbox modes aren't in this preview.

1.1.footerReason: Segment choice is your highest-leverage move right now. It shapes your pricing, channels, and proof points for the next 90 days.

1.2.ianMessage: I need to validate who my customer will be.
1.2.milesResponse: From our last session I pulled three landscaper profiles — each one's a different direction for the lawnmower. Who's jumping out at you?

personas.name: Laura
personas.pain: Gas mowers are loud, clients complain

personas.name: Matt
personas.tooltip: Demo flow continues with Laura — try the highlighted card.

personas.name: Diana
personas.bio: Half-acre lot, mows weekly in season
personas.tooltip: Demo flow continues with Laura — try the highlighted card.

1.3.milesIntro: Before we build, let's pin down the real problem. Five questions — answer what you know, flag what's still a guess. Which one are you least sure about?

questions.a: Gas mowers — loud, fumes, constant maintenance
Honda's brand will obviously win this

questions.hint: Point to evidence, not a feeling…

1.3b.milesIntro: Now the honest part — which of these are hunches? I sorted your claims left to right. Anything on the left is still opinion. Want to defend one, or go test it?
1.3b.log: 3 hunches caught — go talk to people before building.

1.3c.milesIntro: Here's how to turn those hunches into evidence. Not a blank page — a sequence. Start with interviews. Which five landscapers would you call first?

1.3d.milesIntro: Your evidence plan points to customer interviews first. Mary is your sharpest lead — she already flagged the runtime problem in your prototype test. Here's what you know going in.

personaCard.name: Mary
dayInLife: Up at 5 AM, four properties before noon
Truck, trailer, two gas mowers, blower, edger
Books new clients through Facebook and word of mouth
painPoints: First start of the morning wakes the whole neighborhood
Three clients have asked her to switch to electric
wouldPayFor: Same runtime as gas — won't accept a downgrade
whereToFind: Regional trade shows (4 in the next 90 days)

1.3d.milesFollowup: Ready to book that first call? Or want to refine the script first?

bullets: Booked 3 customer conversations for next week
Logged hypothesis: Sole-entrepreneur landscapers will pay a premium for quiet, electric equipment.
1.4.milesFooter: Curious what you'll hear. When you're back from those interviews, we'll head to The Garage.

1.5.milesIntro: Hello again, Ian. From your last conversation, it sounds like supply chain is where you're stuck. How do you want to keep going — meet someone who's solved this, or keep working through your customer personas?

bridgeCard.name: Priya Okafor
bridgeCard.title: Project Manager, Honda R&D
bridgeCard.note: Built e-bike battery supply chain · saves 6 weeks on internal safety review

options.label: Keep working on personas
options.milesReply: Personas are worth a revisit — but the supplier cost is your riskiest open question right now. Let's meet Priya first; you can come back to the personas anytime.

2.1.milesMessage: Ian, Priya looks like a good option, but there are other options as well we can contact.

recommended.name: Priya Okafor
recommended.title: Project Manager, Honda R&D
recommended.reason: Priya solved your exact supplier problem in 2022. She built the e-bike battery supply chain from scratch.

2.1.emptyNetworkMsg: You haven't connected with anyone yet.

actions.label: Browse directory
actions.tooltip: The full directory is not in this demo preview.

2.2.milesMessage: I reached out to Priya — she replied within the hour. Thursday at 2 PM work for you?

meetingCard.title: Project Manager, Honda R&D
meetingCard.context: She's already navigated the supplier and safety review process you're about to hit. She estimates she can save you 6 weeks.

actions.label: Suggest another time
actions.tooltip: Demo flow continues with Thursday 2 PM.

2.3.milesMessage: Good meeting with Priya — I've logged the key takeaways and added her to your network. Anything she said that you want to dig into before we move on?
takeaways: Priya's supplier contacts added to your Key Partners
Battery cost actuals revised: $103/unit (vs. your $140 assumption)
She can save you 6 weeks on the internal safety review

bullets: Connected with Priya — Honda's e-bike battery lead
3 supplier contacts from Priya's network added
Battery cost assumption corrected — $37/unit better than expected
2.4.milesFooter: Next question: do the lawnmower's numbers hold up under pressure?

cta.label: Let's go to the Test Track

3.1.milesMessage: Welcome back. One thing caught my eye in the BMC before the seed review next week — want to take a look?

3.2.milesMessage: Lithium pricing has moved since your February draft — did you catch it? Worth updating your cost of materials before the seed review.

3.3.milesIntro: Before the seed review — want to stress-test this together? Here's what a skeptical CFO is going to push on. Better you hear it from me first.

questions.header: What if Laura's willingness-to-pay is wrong?
questions.challenge: Your 12 interviews skewed toward Lauras who already complained about gas. The broader landscaper market hasn't signaled the same urgency, and your price point sits 22% above the closest competitor.
questions.response: Lead with the three paid pilots — that's revealed preference, not stated. Then acknowledge the sampling skew and show the plan to validate with a colder segment.

questions.header: Your COGS still assumes Honda's supplier pricing. What if we don't get those terms?
questions.challenge: Priya's quotes are favorable because she's been in those relationships for two years. A new venture in year one rarely gets the same pricing tier.
questions.response: Two angles — the venture is internal so the relationships transfer, and here's the margin at standard-tier pricing. Both numbers still clear the hurdle.

questions.header: Sole-entrepreneur landscapers are 12% of the market. Where's the scale story?
questions.challenge: Even if you win 100% of Lauras, that ceiling is too low for Honda's portfolio criteria.
questions.response: Laura is the wedge, not the destination. Show the adjacency path — once the product proves out with sole operators, the case for Matt (commercial fleet) becomes much easier to make internally and externally.

3.4.milesMessage: Seed review is next week — want to see exactly where you stand?
3.4.milesFollowup: 3 of 5 are solid. The two ⚠s are normal at this stage — and there's a plan for each.

scorecard.label: Financials
scorecard.detail: updated, but margin sensitivity still tight

columns.label: The Garage · Month 1
bullets: 4 new contacts in the intrapreneur network

columns.label: The Test Track · Month 3
bullets: Financial model updated with current data

T.1.subhead: Stage-by-stage. Use what you need, when you need it.
T.1.milesCorner: Not sure where to start? Just ask — I'll match you to the right tool.
